summary: Survey judge designs in self-evolve loops + general LLM-judge SOTA; find deficiencies for a purpose-built self-evolve judge

papers.title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models (ACE)
papers.one_liner: Generator/Reflector/Curator loop that grows a structured context 'playbook' from execution feedback, no weight updates.
papers.judge_design: Reward signal is natural execution/environment feedback (code run success/failure, tool outcome, AppWorld task pass). The Reflector is an LLM that reads (trajectory, outcome) and emits natural-language 'lessons' (bullet insights tagged helpful/harmful); the Curator merges them as incremental delta edits to the context. Ground-truth labels used when available; agent mode runs label-free on execution signal only. No dedicated scalar judge, no calibration, one Reflector call per adapted item.
papers.weakness: Paper admits (App. B) effectiveness 'depends critically on feedback quality' and both ACE and DC 'degrade in performance' when GT or reliable execution signals are absent or 'spurious or misleading'; a weak Reflector makes context 'noisy or even harmful.' In tau2-style multi-turn tasks with no execution oracle the only signal is the Reflector's own read of success, so the loop becomes a self-judge with no independent grounding and no drift check across iterations.

papers.title: GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning
papers.one_liner: Reflective genetic-Pareto prompt optimizer that mutates prompts from natural-language reflection on rollout traces.
papers.judge_design: Reward = task metric mu (exact match / F1 / pass-rate, ground-truth-based) that ALSO emits natural-language 'Actionable Side Information' (error messages, profiling, reasoning logs). An LLM reflector reads mu's scalar + traces to do reflective credit assignment and propose targeted prompt edits; a Pareto front of per-instance scores selects candidates. Most rollout budget is spent scoring the validation set purely for candidate selection.
papers.weakness: Entirely dependent on a reliable task metric plus informative traces; paper concedes most rollouts go to validation-set scoring for selection (winner's-curse / overfitting risk) and flags 'feedback engineering' as an open problem. Assumes mu is ground-truthed; in a label-free tau2 setting mu would itself be an LLM judge, which GEPA never stress-tests for gaming or calibration.

papers.title: Training-Free Group Relative Policy Optimization (Training-Free GRPO)
papers.one_liner: Distills a natural-language 'experience' token-prior via group-relative SEMANTIC advantage, zero gradient updates.
papers.judge_design: For verifiable domains a reward model/verifier scores each rollout against ground truth (scalar r_i) and an LLM computes a semantic advantage explaining why rollouts in the group differ. For no-ground-truth tasks (WebWalkerQA) the signal degenerates to the LLM's own implicit majority-voting + self-discrimination + self-reflection across the group, i.e., a self-consensus judge over K rollouts.
papers.weakness: Admits effectiveness is 'dependent on the underlying model's reasoning and tool-use capabilities' (QwQ-32B collapses to 25.5% pass@1); removing ground truth drops AIME24 82.7->80.7. No reward-hacking analysis. The no-GT self-consensus signal is uncalibrated and can converge the whole group toward a shared wrong majority, giving zero learning signal precisely where the agent is systematically mistaken.

papers.title: ReasoningBank: Scaling Agent Self-Evolving with Reasoning Memory
papers.one_liner: Distills reusable reasoning strategies from self-judged success/failure trajectories into retrievable memory (with MaTTS test-time scaling).
papers.judge_design: LLM-as-a-Judge using the SAME backbone as the agent, temperature 0 for determinism, given (user query, trajectory, final website state) -> binary Success/Failure. That single label decides whether a trajectory is distilled into positive or negative memory items (title/description/content). No ground truth at test time (streaming setting). MaTTS scales/diversifies rollouts but grades them all with the same self-judge.
papers.weakness: Paper concedes in App. E it must rely on self-judgment because 'no ground truth is available during test-time,' but gives no error characterization. Judge mislabels silently inject flawed memories that are then retrieved for future tasks, a one-directional contamination loop with no audit or eviction; because the judge shares the agent's backbone it inherits the same blind spots, so systematic failures are never caught.

papers.title: Memento: Fine-tuning LLM Agents without Fine-tuning LLMs
papers.one_liner: Plan-execute agent with a growing Case Bank; learns a case-retrieval Q-policy instead of updating weights.
papers.judge_design: A case is a triplet (state, action, reward). A neural case-selection / soft Q-function is trained by RL on the environment/task reward received per case, steering retrieval toward high-reward past cases. Benchmarks (GAIA, DeepResearcher, SimpleQA, HLE) supply the outcome reward; absent a task oracle the reward must come from an external checker or LLM judge.
papers.weakness: The memory-quality signal is only as good as the per-case reward. In label-free tau2-style settings there is no environment reward to populate Q-values, and low-reward or misjudged cases accumulate in the Case Bank and bias future retrieval. Paper does not analyze poisoned-case accumulation, reward availability at deployment, or what grounds the reward when no verifiable outcome exists.

papers.title: Agent Workflow Memory (AWM)
papers.one_liner: Induces reusable workflows from past trajectories and injects them to guide a web agent; offline or online.
papers.judge_design: Offline: induces workflows from ground-truth-labeled training trajectories. ONLINE (no labels): uses an LM-based evaluator (Pan et al. 2024 WebArena judge) emitting a binary L_eval in {0,1} of whether the self-generated trajectory solved the query; only label-1 trajectories are transformed into workflows via the induction module.
papers.weakness: Explicitly admits 'AWM_online induces workflows from model-predicted trajectories that are not always correct, thus can lead to incorrect workflows that degrade model performance' - classic error propagation where a mistaken judge label writes a bad workflow into memory that cascades to future tasks. The judge is a single frozen binary LLM with no confidence output, no veto, and no way to retract a workflow induced from a false positive.

papers.title: EvoLM: Self-Evolving Language Models through Co-Evolved Discriminative Rubrics
papers.one_liner: Policy and a discriminative-rubric generator co-evolve so the model supplies its own reward without external teachers.
papers.judge_design: A rubric-generator LLM produces explicit discriminative rubrics used as the reward signal, trained discriminatively via pairwise (better/worse) comparisons rather than absolute scalars - the pairwise framing is the stated anti-gaming regularizer. Co-trained with the policy; no human labels, no proprietary judge, no verifiable-domain restriction. (Rubrics outperform GPT-4.1 on RewardBench-2 by 25.7%.)
papers.weakness: Paper admits self-generated criteria 'lack external grounding' and that policy+rubric can enter feedback loops 'optimizing toward spurious metrics,' risking reward collapse/misalignment; states pairwise discrimination is 'not a complete solution' and that extended co-evolution without periodic validation is a vulnerability. The reward provider drifts along with the policy that games it - the canonical self-evolve Goodhart failure, unmitigated.

papers.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
papers.one_liner: Edits a skill.md from scored rollouts, accepting an edit only if it STRICTLY beats a held-out validation score.
papers.judge_design: Reward = automatic benchmark metric on a held-out selection split (extractive-QA score in SearchQA, executable test cases in SpreadsheetBench, exact match, ALFWorld task completion). Strict gate: accept a candidate skill only when its selection-split score is strictly greater than the current score (ties rejected); if it also beats best-seen it is exported as best_skill.md. No LLM judge - relies on task verifiers.
papers.weakness: Admits it is 'most directly applicable when the target task has automatic verifiers, exact-match metrics, executable checks'; open-ended subjective tasks are out of scope, optimized skills 'encode domain-specific heuristics from the training distribution,' and the small held-out split raises data-scarcity concerns. Does not discuss Goodhart/reward hacking - yet strict-greater acceptance on a tiny fixed split across many candidate edits is textbook winner's-curse overfitting to validation noise.

papers.title: Skills-Coach: A Self-Evolving Skill Optimizer via Training-Free GRPO
papers.one_liner: Self-evolving skill optimizer on Training-Free GRPO with comparative-execution and traceable-evaluation modules.
papers.judge_design: Hybrid signal. Deterministic checks for code skills (error-free execution, output/format validity, expected-data match, robustness) PLUS an LLM judge scoring 7 dimensions (structural completeness, practicality, example quality, technical depth, clarity, error handling, comprehensiveness) on 0-100 with supporting evidence; a rule-based 'enhanced heuristic mode' (keyword/structural/statistical checks) is the fallback when the LLM is unavailable. Comparative module scores original vs optimized skill.
papers.weakness: The dual-mode fallback tacitly concedes LLM-judge unreliability but the paper gives NO explicit treatment of reward hacking, self-judge circularity (optimizer and judge share a model family), or the 7-dimension rubric being gamed. The absolute 0-100 scores are uncalibrated and drift as the skill population changes, and semantic-advantage optimization pushes edits directly against that unstable rubric.

papers.title: SkillCoach: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Evaluating and Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use
papers.one_liner: Self-evolving trajectory-level rubrics that grade agentic skill-use, with validation-gated rubric patches.
papers.judge_design: External LLM judge (Gemini 3.1 Pro) applies the current rubric R_t to trajectory evidence (skill reads, tool calls, file edits, script runs, artifacts) - positive judgments must cite event evidence - producing a weighted process score S_meta (skill selection 40% / following 30% / composition 20% / reflection 10%) kept SEPARATE from an external outcome verifier s_ver applied post-hoc. An arbitration model proposes localized rubric patches accepted only through a hard destructiveness gate + soft objective improvement > 0.2 + material validation signal + structural-integrity check.
papers.weakness: Paper admits it does NOT test reward hacking, specification gaming, or rubric drift, validates rubric quality only against human-gold references on a small skill set, and does not address on-policy RL or long-term deployment feedback. Because the rubric is edited by the same arbitration system whose scores it produces, under an optimizer there is no frozen reference to detect a rubric being progressively loosened toward the policy.

papers.title: RewardHarness: Self-Evolving Agentic Post-Training
papers.one_liner: Co-evolves a versioned library of grading Skills (rubrics) and Tools for reward modeling, gated on held-out accuracy.
papers.judge_design: Reward = ground-truth HUMAN preference labels on a held-out validation split (calibration set: 60 train / 40 val). A library update is accepted only if validation accuracy improves over the current best, else rolled back. Co-evolving artifacts are declarative markdown rubric Skills (scoring criteria + examples) and procedural visual-analysis Tools (invocation conditions + protocols).
papers.weakness: Explicitly admits the learned library 'can still over-specialize to recurring failure modes if the demonstration set is narrow or biased,' and the conservative rollback gate 'does not guarantee discovery of globally optimal Skills or Tools' (rejects edits that help rare cases but slightly hurt frequent ones). No safeguard against reward hacking / adversarial specification evolution; a 40-example validation split is an extremely thin gate for many candidate edits, inviting selection on noise.

papers.title: On Safety Risks in Experience-Driven Self-Evolving Agents
papers.one_liner: Shows benign experience accumulation itself degrades safety in self-evolving agents (no adversary required).
papers.judge_design: Not a method paper: analyzes the experience/retrieval loop rather than a scalar judge. Identifies an 'execution bias' where retrieved experience over-reinforces execution-oriented behaviors; the signal studied is the semantic content of stored trajectories, not a graded reward.
papers.weakness: For judge design its key finding is that the evaluation-and-memory loop drifts unsafely even under benign accumulation - relevant because a self-evolve judge sits on top of exactly this drifting substrate. It stops short of modeling deliberate self-judge reward hacking or memory poisoning of the eval signal and proposes no concrete defense (only vague 'safety-utility trade-offs'), leaving the judge-corruption threat model open.

papers.title: EvoTrainer: Co-Evolving LLM Policies and Training Harnesses for Autonomous Agentic RL
papers.one_liner: Co-evolves the LLM policy AND its training harness (including the evaluation apparatus) for autonomous agentic RL.
papers.judge_design: Task-specific reward computed inside a training harness that itself iteratively improves alongside the policy (RLVR-style verifiable rewards plus harness-generated evaluation). The evaluation apparatus, not just the policy, is part of the evolving system, so the reference frame that defines 'correct' is mutable.
papers.weakness: When the harness that computes the reward is co-evolved by/with the agent, there is no fixed anchor for correctness; the paper makes no explicit admission or defense about the co-evolved reward being hackable or drifting - which is precisely the Goodhart failure mode when an optimizer can shape both the policy and the thing that grades it.

papers.title: MetaSkill-Evolve: Recursive Self-Improvement of LLM Agents via Two-Timescale Meta-Skill Evolution
papers.one_liner: Two-timescale recursive self-improvement: fast skill evolution + slow meta-skill evolution over an archive of scored children.
papers.judge_design: Utility U(s) = E[r(A_s(x), y)] with r in [0,1], estimated as accuracy on a held-out validation batch. A generated child skill enters the archive as an eligible parent only if it shows positive improvement (Delta U > 0). The same held-out-accuracy signal drives both the fast and slow timescales.
papers.weakness: Admits transfer to 'open-ended, long-horizon real-world tasks with noisier feedback is untested'; on saturated benchmarks (ALFWorld 92%+) the validation split must be enlarged or 'the evolution loop has nothing to learn from and cannot improve' (feedback starvation). Recursive updates depend entirely on one imperfect verifiable signal, so errors can compound across timescales, and it never uses any judge on non-verifiable outputs.

papers.title: SkillAxe: Sharpening LLM-Authored Agent Skills Through Evaluation-Guided Self-Refinement
papers.one_liner: Fully-unsupervised evaluation-guided self-refinement of LLM-authored skills.
papers.judge_design: The LLM self-diagnoses each skill along 4 interpretable dimensions (quality impact, trigger precision, instruction compliance with fault attribution, solution-path coverage) and emits structured 'improvement briefs.' Explicitly requires 'no ground-truth labels, test suites, or environment rewards' - a pure self-judge producing natural-language diagnoses.
papers.weakness: With zero external anchor the entire signal is the model grading its own skills; the paper does not characterize how these self-diagnoses correlate with real task success or resist the model's own blind spots. This is an ideal Goodhart substrate: refiner and judge share weights and there is no falsification channel, so a skill can be 'sharpened' toward the judge's preferences while regressing on the actual task.

papers.title: CoEvoSkills: Self-Evolving Agent Skills via Co-Evolutionary Verification
papers.one_liner: Skill generator and a surrogate verifier co-evolve to author multi-file skill packages without gold tests.
papers.judge_design: A surrogate verifier co-evolves with the skill generator to provide 'informative and actionable feedback without access to ground-truth test content'; the verifier is itself learned/evolved rather than fixed, and grades the multi-file skills the generator produces.
papers.weakness: Because the verifier is co-evolved and has no access to gold tests, its feedback can drift toward whatever the generator finds easy to satisfy. The paper does not address verifier reliability or reward hacking - an evolving verifier with no external anchor is directly vulnerable to being gamed by the generator it grades, the same co-evolution collapse risk EvoLM and EvoTrainer expose.

papers.title: Agent Skill Evaluation and Evolution: Frameworks and Benchmarks
papers.one_liner: Survey/benchmark of how skill evolution is evaluated; catalogs the signal-granularity gaps in the field.
papers.judge_design: Not a judge itself: categorizes signals as step-level execution feedback, sequence-level trajectory distillation, and task-level RL reward, and observes evaluation is 'predominantly binary (pass/fail).' Frames the design space rather than implementing a judge.
papers.weakness: Documents that NO existing benchmark tracks skill improvement longitudinally across multiple rounds of feedback, and that binary pass/fail overlooks token cost, latency, and reasoning quality. In other words the field lacks any evaluation measuring whether judge-driven evolution actually compounds over iterations; it calls for composite rewards it does not build, and never quantifies LLM-judge reliability under optimizer pressure.

deficiency_patterns: Self-judge circularity: the evaluator is the same (or same-family) backbone as the agent/optimizer it grades (ReasoningBank temp-0 self-judge, EvoLM/CoEvoSkills co-evolved rubric/verifier, Training-Free GRPO no-GT self-consensus, SkillAxe fully self-diagnosed), so the reward inherits the optimizer's blind spots and there is no independent signal to falsify a systematic error the optimizer is exploiting.

Point-estimate accept gates with no statistical control: edits are accepted on 'strictly greater than' a single held-out score against a tiny fixed split (SkillOpt strict >, RewardHarness 40-example val, MetaSkill-Evolve Delta U>0, GEPA validation-selection), so thousands of candidate edits guarantee selection on validation noise (winner's curse) with no confidence interval or multiple-comparison correction.

No drift/recalibration mechanism: judges and rubrics are treated as stationary while the trajectory distribution they score is deliberately shifted by the optimizer; even SkillCoach, which evolves its rubric, admits it never tests rubric drift, so the grading scale silently moves with the policy that games it.

Reward-hacking is admitted-away, not measured: nearly every method (ACE, Skills-Coach, SkillOpt, Training-Free GRPO, CoEvoSkills, EvoTrainer) states no adversarial/Goodhart test of the judge under optimizer pressure, so the loop simply assumes the judge is not being gamed at the exact point where it is most likely to be.

Binary or coarse verdicts hide partial-credit hacking and operational regressions: success/failure labels (AWM, ReasoningBank) and outcome-only checks cannot separate a genuinely-solved task from a superficially-satisfying one, and the survey (2606.11435) confirms cost/latency/reasoning-quality are invisible to pass/fail signals used as reward.

Entangled artifact-and-evaluator: when the same optimizer can edit both the policy context and the grading rubric/verifier (SkillCoach, EvoLM, CoEvoSkills, RewardHarness, EvoTrainer), there is no frozen reference to detect specification gaming - the rubric can be loosened toward the policy rather than the policy improved toward the rubric.

No calibrated abstention or uncertainty: judges emit a point verdict/score (0-100, binary, or process-weight) with no 'I don't know,' so low-confidence and high-confidence judgments enter the accept gate with equal weight, and near-threshold noise is indistinguishable from real improvement.

One-directional, irreversible contamination: a mislabeled trajectory becomes a permanent memory/skill/case (ReasoningBank, AWM online, Memento) that then biases future judgments and retrieval, with no retro-audit or eviction, and 2604.16968 shows even benign accumulation drifts the loop.

Judge cost is not budgeted against call volume: designs assume thousands of judge calls per run yet route every trajectory to a single expensive frontier judge (SkillCoach on Gemini 3.1 Pro per trajectory) with no cheap-first triage, disagreement-triggered escalation, or amortization.

Signal only exists where a verifier exists: the strongest gates (SkillOpt, MetaSkill-Evolve, GEPA, RewardHarness) depend on automatic verifiers / ground-truth splits and explicitly degrade or become inapplicable on open-ended tau2-style multi-turn tasks with no execution oracle - exactly the target setting.
open_slots: An optimizer-frozen, deliberately heterogeneous judge: an evaluator drawn from a different model family than the agent/optimizer and held fixed against the optimization pressure, periodically re-anchored to a small human/verifier gold set - none of these loops uses an independent, separately-sourced judge inside the loop.

Statistically-gated acceptance: replace 'strictly greater than' on a point estimate with sequential hypothesis testing / false-discovery-rate control over the thousands of edit proposals, so validation-noise winners are rejected by construction.

A Goodhart/drift canary: track the verdict distribution of the live judge against a frozen reference judge on a held-out gold set the optimizer never sees, and auto-halt or roll back when divergence exceeds a threshold - proactive drift detection rather than post-hoc audit.

Calibrated-abstention judging: emit verdict plus a calibrated confidence, let only edits whose supporting judgments clear a confidence threshold pass the gate, and quarantine (not memorize) abstained trajectories - no surveyed method carries uncertainty into the accept decision.

Disagreement-gated ensembles: accept an edit only when independent heterogeneous judges agree, so exploiting one judge's idiosyncrasy is insufficient to move the reward - unused in these loops, which all rely on a single judge or self-consensus.

Reversible, audited memory: a scheduled retro-verification pass that re-judges already-accepted skills/cases/workflows against fresh evidence and evicts poisoned entries, closing the one-directional contamination loop that ReasoningBank/AWM/Memento leave open.

Cost-aware cascaded judging sized to the call budget: deterministic checks and a cheap small-model triage first, escalating to an expensive judge only on disagreement or near-threshold cases - no method budgets judge cost against the thousands-of-calls-per-run reality.

Paired/counterfactual delta judging: score the difference between original and edited trajectory on matched inputs rather than absolute scores, neutralizing the judge's absolute-scale drift under the optimizer-induced distribution shift.

An explicit process-vs-outcome consistency veto: flag and reject trajectories where the process judge looks good but the outcome verifier fails (a built-in reward-hacking detector) instead of averaging the two - SkillCoach keeps the two signals separate but never uses their disagreement as a veto.

A meta-judge that audits rubric edits for specification gaming: a rubric-diff reviewer that must approve each proposed rubric/verifier change before it is committed, applying drift-attack defenses proactively inside the loop rather than only studying the attack offline.

papers.title: Incentivizing Agentic Reasoning in LLM Judges via Tool-Integrated Reinforcement Learning (TIR-Judge)
papers.one_liner: An 8B judge that calls a code executor mid-verdict and is trained by iterative RL without distillation, matching Claude-Opus-4 on listwise judging.
papers.judge_design: Single agentic judge (8B) that interleaves reasoning with tool calls (a code executor for precise/verifiable checks) before emitting a verdict; supports pointwise, pairwise, and listwise output formats. Trained end-to-end with iterative RL (GRPO-style) that bootstraps from the base model (TIR-Judge-Zero variant uses no distilled trajectories). Reward is correctness against verifiable and non-verifiable benchmark labels. Reported gains: +6.4% pointwise, +7.7% pairwise over reasoning-only judges; listwise comparable to Opus-4. Cost: multiple forward passes plus sandboxed code execution per judgment.
papers.weakness: Tool execution latency and sandbox cost make it unaffordable at the thousands-of-calls-per-run frequency of a self-evolve loop; the code-executor advantage only exists on the verifiable slice, so on non-verifiable tau2-style trajectory quality it degenerates to a text judge. Worse, because it was RL-trained to maximize benchmark reward, an optimizer sharing the same tool interface can craft trajectories whose code-checkable surface looks correct while the semantic goal fails, i.e. the tool becomes a gameable proxy.

papers.title: RM-R1: Reward Modeling as Reasoning
papers.one_liner: Reward model that first classifies the input, then self-generates a chain-of-rubrics, then scores against it.
papers.judge_design: Generative reward model trained in two stages: reasoning distillation followed by RL from verifiable rewards. At inference it runs a 'Chain-of-Rubrics' procedure — categorize the sample type, synthesize task-specific evaluation rubrics on the fly, then reason to a preference/score. Output is a structured rationale plus verdict. Calibration is implicit (RL-tuned); cost is a long CoT generation per judgment.
papers.weakness: The rubric is regenerated per call and is never audited, so in a loop the optimizer's outputs steer which rubric the judge invents — a form of prompt-conditioned rubric drift where the policy elicits lenient self-generated criteria. Long-CoT cost is prohibitive at loop frequency, and the self-generated rubric gives Goodhart pressure a moving-but-policy-controlled target rather than a fixed defensible standard.

papers.title: J1: Incentivizing Thinking in LLM-as-a-Judge via Reinforcement Learning
papers.one_liner: RL-trained thinking judge (8B-70B) that produces long deliberation before a verdict, SOTA on RewardBench/JudgeBench/PPE.
papers.judge_design: Single-model generative judge trained with online RL to emit extended chain-of-thought before a pairwise/pointwise verdict. Reward encourages verifiable-correct verdicts; trained on Llama-3.1 and Qwen3 at 8B/32B/70B. Output is deliberation + decision. No explicit uncertainty or abstention; calibration only via RL.
papers.weakness: Optimizes verdict accuracy on static preference benchmarks, not robustness to an adversary that moves the response distribution — nothing in the objective penalizes being confidently wrong on out-of-distribution optimizer-generated trajectories. It emits a hard verdict with no abstention channel, so a self-evolve loop cannot tell a confident judgment from a coin-flip, and the long CoT makes thousands of calls costly.

papers.title: Multi-Agent Debate for LLM Judges with Adaptive Stability Detection
papers.one_liner: A 7-judge debate panel with a Beta-Binomial/KS-statistic early-stop that cuts rounds from 10 to 2-8.
papers.judge_design: Panel of ~7 LLM judge agents debate over up to T=10 rounds: round 0 independent judgments, then each agent revises seeing peers' verdicts and rationales. A time-varying Beta-Binomial mixture (EM-fit) models consensus dynamics; a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (KS<0.05 for two consecutive rounds) triggers early termination. Final verdict is consensus if unanimous else majority vote. Gains: +2-5% on hard tasks (JudgeBench/LLMBar) over majority voting; cost is 7 agents x several rounds.
papers.weakness: The paper itself lists consensus bias, collusion, and sycophancy: debate converges to a shared wrong prior, and in a self-evolve loop the optimizer can find inputs that trip the shared blind spot of all seven agents (correlated pretraining), so debate amplifies rather than cancels the exploitable error. Cost is far too high for high-frequency reward calls, and the stability detector can early-stop on a false consensus that the optimizer specifically induced.

papers.title: From Many Voices to One: Statistically Principled Aggregation of LLM Judges
papers.one_liner: Reliability-weighted (Dawid-Skene-style) aggregation of heterogeneous judges that treats inter-judge disagreement as calibrated epistemic uncertainty.
papers.judge_design: Ensemble of heterogeneous judges whose votes are combined by a statistical latent-truth model that estimates per-judge reliability (sensitivity/specificity) rather than naive majority. Output is an aggregated verdict plus a disagreement-derived uncertainty. Key claim: diverse imperfect judges beat correlated 'perfect' ones; disagreement is a usable uncertainty signal. Label requirement is light (reliability estimated from agreement structure).
papers.weakness: The reliability estimates and the independence assumption are fit on a static snapshot; under optimizer pressure the errors that were independent become correlated (the optimizer targets the shared failure mode), collapsing the variance-reduction guarantee. Because reliability weights are frozen during optimization, the aggregate has a fixed exploitable structure and cannot detect that a whole sub-panel is now being systematically fooled.

papers.title: Alternating RL for Rubric-Based Reward Modeling in Non-Verifiable LLM Post-Training (RubricARM)
papers.one_liner: Co-evolves the rubric-based reward model with the policy: alternate policy-RL steps with rubric-refinement steps that patch discovered exploits.
papers.judge_design: Multi-dimensional rubric reward model scoring responses across explicit criteria. Training alternates two phases: (1) optimize policy against current rubric; (2) refine the rubric by identifying responses where the policy exploited scoring weaknesses and revising criteria to close them. Output is per-dimension scores. The alternation is the anti-Goodhart mechanism — the reward target is intentionally non-stationary.
papers.weakness: Rubric refinement requires detecting exploits, but the paper leaves that detection under-specified and partly manual; if the refinement lags the policy, the loop converges on an already-exploited criterion before it is patched. It targets verifiable-ish post-training, not label-free multi-turn tool trajectories, where 'which criterion did the policy game' is far harder to localize. This is the closest existing design to what a self-evolve judge needs, but it presumes a labeled/exploit-detectable signal the tau2-style no-label setting lacks.

papers.title: AutoChecklist: Composable Pipelines for Checklist Generation and Scoring with LLM-as-a-Judge
papers.one_liner: Decomposes holistic judging into an auto-generated per-instance checklist, then scores each yes/no item independently.
papers.judge_design: Two-stage composable pipeline: a generator produces an instance-specific checklist of binary criteria from the instruction, then a scorer LLM answers each checklist item independently and aggregates to a score. Output is a checklist with per-item verdicts plus an aggregate. Interpretable and modular; cost scales with checklist length (one judge call per item or one batched call).
papers.weakness: The checklist is generated once from the instruction and is blind to the trajectory, so it cannot catch tool-use process failures that no static item enumerates; item independence means the optimizer can satisfy every listed item while missing the unlisted true goal (checklist-completeness Goodhart). In a loop, the same checklist is reused thousands of times, giving the policy a fully enumerable, thus fully hackable, target surface.

papers.title: RubricEval: A Rubric-Level Meta-Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Judges in Instruction Following
papers.one_liner: Meta-eval benchmark that scores judges at the granularity of individual rubric items, not just final verdicts.
papers.judge_design: Not a judge but a meta-evaluation harness: provides instances with expert-written instance-specific rubrics and measures how well an LLM judge assigns the correct per-rubric-item score, isolating which criteria judges mis-apply. Output metric is rubric-item-level agreement with human standards.
papers.weakness: It measures static human agreement on held-out instructions, not judge behavior under adversarial optimization — a judge topping RubricEval can still be trivially gamed inside a loop. It offers no hackability/robustness axis, so using it to select the loop's judge optimizes the wrong quantity; and it covers single-turn instruction following, not multi-turn tool trajectories.

papers.title: SCOPE: Selective Conformal Optimized Pairwise LLM Judging
papers.one_liner: Wraps a pairwise judge in conformal risk control with a position-bias-neutral entropy estimator to guarantee <=alpha error among accepted verdicts, abstaining otherwise.
papers.judge_design: Two-stage: (1) Bidirectional Preference Entropy (BPE) — query the judge in both orderings, average the two probabilities, take binary entropy as an order-stable uncertainty score; (2) conformal risk control picks threshold lambda-hat on a labeled 50% calibration split so the false-discovery rate among accepted (non-abstained) verdicts satisfies FDR<=alpha under exchangeability. Output: {A, B, abstain}. Cost: two forward passes per pair; needs white-box logits and ground-truth calibration labels; empirical FDR ~0.097 at alpha=0.10 with up to 2.4x coverage of naive baselines.
papers.weakness: The FDR guarantee holds only under exchangeability between calibration and test — but a self-evolve optimizer deliberately shifts the trajectory distribution every iteration, voiding the guarantee exactly when relied on. Abstention rises on the novel/hard trajectories the optimizer pushes toward, so coverage collapses at the frontier and the loop is starved of signal on precisely the exploratory moves it needs to score. It also requires labeled calibration data the no-label optimization setting does not have, and is pairwise-only.

papers.title: Analyzing Uncertainty of LLM-as-a-Judge: Interval Evaluations with Conformal Prediction
papers.one_liner: Turns scalar judge scores into conformal prediction intervals with a distribution-free coverage guarantee.
papers.judge_design: Applies split conformal prediction to a scoring judge: a nonconformity score (e.g. |predicted - true| on a calibration set) yields a threshold so the judge outputs an interval/prediction-set [L,U] of ratings containing the true score with probability >=1-alpha. Output is an interval rather than a point; low added compute beyond calibration; needs a labeled calibration set.
papers.weakness: Interval width grows with task difficulty and judge confusion — so on the hardest optimizer-explored trajectories the judge returns a near-useless wide interval, giving the loop no usable gradient exactly when adaptation matters most (the authors note this friction explicitly). A scalar loop needs a point reward; collapsing the interval (e.g. to its midpoint) discards the guarantee, and the coverage guarantee again assumes exchangeability the optimizer breaks.

papers.title: Calibrating LLM Judges: Linear Probes for Fast and Reliable Uncertainty Estimation
papers.one_liner: Trains a cheap linear probe on judge hidden states to predict its own correctness, avoiding 10-20x ensemble cost.
papers.judge_design: A lightweight linear probe over the judge model's internal hidden-state activations, trained to predict whether the judge's verdict is correct; produces a fast per-verdict confidence/uncertainty score without multiple samples. Motivated by verbalized confidence being overconfident and ensembles costing 10-20x. Needs white-box access and a small labeled probe-training set.
papers.weakness: The probe is trained on the pre-loop activation distribution; as the optimizer drives the policy off-distribution, the probe's own reliability degrades silently and undetectably (no guarantee, unlike conformal). It requires white-box access to the judge, ruling out API judges, and the labeled correctness data needed to fit the probe is unavailable at optimization time in the label-free setting.

papers.title: Two Samples Are Enough: Verbal Confidence Meets Self-Consistency in Reasoning LLMs
papers.one_liner: Hybridizes verbalized confidence with self-consistency and shows two samples suffice for strong calibration.
papers.judge_design: Confidence estimator that combines the model's verbalized confidence with self-consistency across a tiny number (as few as 2) of stochastic samples, aggregating agreement + stated confidence into a calibrated score. Cheap (2 forward passes) relative to large ensembles; black-box compatible; no calibration labels strictly required.
papers.weakness: Self-consistency measures the judge's internal stability, not correctness — an optimizer can produce trajectories on which the judge is consistently and confidently wrong (shared systematic bias), and this estimator will report high confidence, actively certifying the exploit. Two-sample consistency is a weak signal precisely on the ambiguous boundary cases the optimizer concentrates on.

papers.title: FairJudge: An Adaptive, Debiased, and Consistent LLM-as-a-Judge
papers.one_liner: Trains one judge to be self-consistent across pointwise and pairwise protocols, using cross-mode agreement as a debiasing reward.
papers.judge_design: Single judge trained to produce both a pointwise score and a pairwise preference for the same instance, with a consistency reward aligning the two modes plus explicit position/length debiasing (order swapping, length controls). Output covers both protocols; adaptivity selects protocol per instance. Mitigates the documented pointwise-vs-pairwise divergence.
papers.weakness: Cross-mode consistency is orthogonal to correctness — a judge can be perfectly self-consistent and consistently biased, and the optimizer will exploit the shared bias in both modes at once. The debiasing targets position/length/style biases catalogued on static benchmarks, but a self-evolve loop invents new, distribution-specific biases (e.g. tool-call verbosity, apology phrasing) that no pre-registered debiasing control covers.

papers.title: ToolPRMBench: Evaluating and Advancing Process Reward Models for Tool-using Agents
papers.one_liner: Step-level PRM benchmark plus trained ToolPRMs that pick the correct next action; general PRMs score ~50% (chance) on tool tasks.
papers.judge_design: Each case is (history h_t, correct action a+, plausible-wrong action a-, tool metadata m_t); the PRM does binary discrimination of which candidate action is correct. Three trained variants: ToolPRM-Base (SFT classifier), -CoT (reasoning then choose), -GRPO (RL with binary 1/0 reward). 984 cases over ToolTalk/GTA/BFCL/ToolSandbox; labels built by 3-model majority vote + human review (96% agreement). Output is a step-level preference/score.
papers.weakness: The paper's own finding is fatal for loop use: PRMs below ~50% accuracy 'yield negative gains' and actively harm the policy during reward-guided search, and SFT PRMs drop 20-21% out-of-distribution — which is exactly the regime an exploring optimizer creates. Binary 0/1 rewards are too sparse for credit assignment across a multi-step trajectory, and synthetic-error training helps one benchmark (+22% GTA) while hurting another, so the reward signal is unreliable and non-transferable under distribution shift.

papers.title: AgentProcessBench: Diagnosing Step-Level Process Quality in Tool-Using Agents
papers.one_liner: First benchmark for judging intermediate-step quality (+1/0/-1) in agent trajectories; SOTA judges hit only 67-72% agreement vs 89% human.
papers.judge_design: Each assistant step is labeled +1 (advances task), 0 (neutral/exploratory), or -1 (harmful/erroneous), with an error-propagation rule marking downstream steps -1 until explicitly corrected. Judge input is the full trajectory (system/user/assistant/tool messages); it must score each step without hindsight bias and output JSON of per-step labels + reasoning + final outcome. Metrics: StepAcc and FirstErrAcc (earliest critical failure).
papers.weakness: The paper enumerates the exact biases that become directional optimization pressure if this is used as reward: positive-label bias (reinforces wrong behavior), neutral-step confusion (punishes legitimate exploration), and length-penalty accumulation (biases toward premature termination). The error-propagation rule traps agents into penalizing entire recoverable branches. StepAcc falls 3.8-13.5 points on the longest trajectories, and 67-72% human agreement means the dense signal is too noisy for reliable self-improvement supervision.

papers.title: AgentPRM: Process Reward Models for LLM Agents via Step-Wise Promise and Progress (WWW 2026)
papers.one_liner: Dense agent PRM built from two bootstrapped signals — 'promise' (expected future success from a state) and 'progress' (advancement toward the goal).
papers.judge_design: Process reward model producing a dense per-step reward decomposed into a 'promise' term (value-style estimate of eventual success from the current state, from Monte-Carlo/rollout estimates) and a 'progress' term (incremental advancement toward the goal). Trained on the agent's own trajectories with outcome-derived labels; output is a scalar step reward used to guide search / RL over long horizons.
papers.weakness: Promise/progress are bootstrapped from the current policy's rollouts, so the reward inherits the policy's blind spots — in a self-evolve loop it certifies as 'promising' the very stalling behaviors the policy already favors, a self-reinforcing bias with no external anchor. Value-style estimates are notoriously exploitable: the optimizer can inflate estimated promise without real progress (looks-good-goes-nowhere trajectories), and the reward provides no abstention or uncertainty channel to flag when its state-value estimate is off-distribution.

papers.title: UCCI: Calibrated Uncertainty for Cost-Optimal LLM Cascade Routing
papers.one_liner: Routes each query through a small-then-large judge cascade using calibrated uncertainty to hit a cost-optimal accept/escalate point.
papers.judge_design: Cascade of judges (cheap first) with a calibrated uncertainty estimator deciding accept-vs-escalate per query; the routing threshold is chosen to be cost-optimal for a target accuracy. Signals include confidence estimators, token probabilities, self-consistency. Output is the accepted judge's verdict; cost is amortized because most queries clear the cheap tier.
papers.weakness: The routing decision rests on an uncertainty estimate that is only weakly correlated with correctness (comparable work reports AUC~0.60), and an optimizer can craft trajectories that the cheap judge is confidently-wrong on, so they never escalate — the cascade systematically leaks exactly the gamed cases to the cheapest, most hackable tier. Calibration of the router assumes a fixed query distribution that the loop violates.

papers.title: On Cost-Effective LLM-as-a-Judge Improvement Techniques
papers.one_liner: Distills a large teacher judge into a mini judge and adds criteria + variance-gated escalation for 85.8% accuracy at 1.3x cost.
papers.judge_design: Compares four cost levers: k=8 ensemble scoring, task-specific one-line criteria appended to the prompt, calibration-context (in-context scored reference examples to anchor the scale), and adaptive model escalation using per-response score variance as the routing signal (hard/soft/variance-informed variants). Distills GPT-5 teacher into a small judge (e.g. Qwen3-8B). Best config: mini+criteria at k=8, 85.8% (+13.5pp) at 1.3x baseline cost; mini k=8 matches full-model k=8 at ~1/4 cost.
papers.weakness: The distilled mini judge is the most Goodhart-vulnerable component — it is a compressed proxy of a proxy, and repeatedly selecting training data with it under optimization pressure amplifies its blind spots (the paper acknowledges it never tests recursive/high-frequency deployment). The variance escalation signal is weak (AUC~0.60, r=-0.13 with correctness), so the gate cannot reliably catch the adversarial cases; criteria injection gives the optimizer a small, enumerable target surface to satisfy.

papers.title: Judge's Verdict: A Comprehensive Analysis of LLM Judge Capability Through Human Agreement
papers.one_liner: Meta-eval taxonomy that scores judges by human-agreement type, distinguishing genuine alignment from superficial correlation.
papers.judge_design: Meta-evaluation methodology (not a judge): a two-step human-agreement analysis that classifies judges by how their scores correlate with humans, separating 'human-like' judges from those achieving correlation via spurious patterns. Produces a capability ranking across judge models.
papers.weakness: Human agreement on a static i.i.d. corpus is a poor predictor of loop safety — it never stresses the judge with optimizer-generated adversarial trajectories, so a top-ranked judge here can still be trivially hacked. It offers no hackability, drift-robustness, or abstention-quality axis, which are the properties that actually determine whether a judge survives thousands of Goodhart-pressured calls; using this ranking to pick the loop's judge optimizes the wrong objective.

deficiency_patterns: Exchangeability-dependent guarantees break under the optimizer: every conformal/selective-prediction judge (SCOPE, interval-conformal) certifies its FDR/coverage bound only if calibration and test data are exchangeable, but a self-evolve optimizer deliberately shifts the trajectory distribution every iteration, so the guarantee is voided exactly in the regime it is invoked. No surveyed design re-calibrates online against optimizer-induced covariate shift.

Abstention starves the frontier: uncertainty/abstention mechanisms (SCOPE, interval judges, cascade gates) raise their abstain/wide-interval rate on the novel, hard trajectories the optimizer pushes toward, so the loop receives least signal on precisely the exploratory moves it must score, biasing selection toward easy already-solved regions and suppressing exploration.

Static judge vs stationary target: with the sole exception of RubricARM's alternating rubric refinement, every judge (distilled mini-judge, frozen PRM, RL-trained generative judge, reused checklist/rubric) is held fixed while the optimizer attacks it for thousands of steps, giving Goodhart pressure a stationary, fully-probeable surface; the weak uncertainty proxies protecting it (AUC~0.60, r~-0.13 with correctness) provide no defense.

Process/step-level judges encode biases that become directional pressure: step PRMs (ToolPRMBench, AgentProcessBench, AgentPRM) have low human agreement (67-72%) and systematic positive-label bias, neutral-step confusion, error-propagation, and length penalties; used as dense reward these do not merely add noise, they steer the policy to suppress exploration, terminate prematurely, and abandon recoverable branches.

Ensemble/debate independence assumption collapses under attack: panel, debate, and statistical-aggregation designs derive their variance reduction from (near-)independent judge errors, but an optimizer searches for inputs that hit the shared blind spot inherited from common pretraining, correlating the errors and nullifying the benefit; debate additionally converges via collusion/sycophancy to a shared wrong prior.

Reliability and affordability trade off with no joint budget for the loop frequency: the cheapest judges (distilled minis) are the most hackable, the most robust judges (tool-using TIR-Judge, 7-agent debate, long-CoT RM-R1/J1) are unaffordable at thousands of calls per run, and cascades that try to bridge the two route on uncertainty signals that are themselves gameable and un-calibrated under drift.

Meta-evaluation measures the wrong quantity: every meta-benchmark (RubricEval, Judge's Verdict, RewardBench/JudgeBench-style) scores judges by static human agreement on i.i.d. held-out data; none measures robustness under adversarial optimization (reward-gap growth / hackability), so 'SOTA judge' rankings do not predict which judge survives a self-evolve loop, and selecting on them optimizes for the wrong property.

Verdicts are scalarized and opaque to the optimizer: judges emit a score or preference but not a legible, structured statement of which criterion is unresolved or being gamed; verbalized confidence is overconfident, probe-based confidence needs white-box access and pre-loop calibration data, and none feeds back a targeted 'this rubric item is the open exploit' signal the optimizer could be constrained to address, so the reward stays a hackable black box.
open_slots: Training-free optimizer-aware judge that co-adapts online: RubricARM shows co-evolving the rubric with the policy defeats a static target, but it needs RL and exploit-detection labels; nobody builds a label-free version that, between self-evolve iterations, mines the distribution of newly-accepted trajectories to detect an emerging exploit and patches the rubric/criteria in place — a 'judge that moves when the target moves' for the no-label tau2 setting.

Drift-conditional (covariate-shift) conformal calibration for judges: re-estimate the abstention threshold each iteration against the current policy's trajectory distribution using weighted/online conformal prediction, so the FDR/coverage guarantee survives optimizer-induced shift instead of being silently voided. No surveyed conformal judge does anything but one static calibration split.

Hackability as a first-class judge metric and selection criterion: instead of ranking judges by static human agreement, measure reward-gap growth under a dedicated red-team optimizer and pick/weight judges by their resistance to gaming. No meta-benchmark or judge-selection method in this sweep operationalizes adversarial-optimization robustness.

Adversarially-decorrelated panel with label-free reliability weights: explicitly engineer judge members to have disjoint blind spots (different tool access, different rubric decompositions, different base families) and estimate reliability weights without labels from cross-judge disagreement anchored to cheap verifiable checks, so no single optimizer-crafted input fools all members. Current panels (debate, PoLL, statistical aggregation) assume rather than engineer independence.

Per-step verifier-anchored cascade for trajectories: gate the expensive LLM/PRM reward behind cheap deterministic checks at every step (GRASP-style counts, tool-call schema validity, unit tests) and spend LLM-judge budget only where the verifiable layer is silent. Existing cascades (UCCI, cost-effective escalation) route on weak learned uncertainty, not on a deterministic-verifiable floor tuned to the no-label loop.

Abstention-as-exploration-signal rather than veto: route trajectories the judge abstains on into an active-labeling / self-consistency-escalation / human-in-the-loop queue instead of dropping them, so the loop keeps learning at the frontier. Every selective-prediction judge here treats abstention as a terminal veto that removes signal exactly where the optimizer is exploring.

Structured unresolved-criterion feedback channel: have the judge return not a scalar but which specific rubric item is uncertain or violated, and constrain the optimizer to make targeted edits addressing that item — making the reward legible closes the Goodhart gap that scalarization opens. No surveyed judge exposes its uncertainty in an optimizer-actionable, per-criterion form.

Iteration-scheduled judge budget: allocate cheap-judge vs expensive-tool/debate-judge calls as a function of loop progress — spend more verification budget late, when the policy is most optimized and most likely to be gaming — instead of a fixed per-call judge design. Cost-efficiency papers optimize a single static operating point, never a schedule across the run.

Recoverability-aware trajectory judge: score whether a later step repairs an earlier error rather than propagating -1 forward, removing the exploration-suppression and premature-termination bias that AgentProcessBench/ToolPRMBench-style error-propagation rules bake into the reward. No process judge in this sweep credits recovery.

papers.title: When RLHF Fails: A Mechanistic Taxonomy of Reward Hacking, Collapse, and Evaluator Gaming
papers.one_liner: Reframes judge failure as a training-dynamics phenomenon: reward hacking, collapse, and evaluator gaming can be localized to individual prompts and checkpoints and partially anticipated, but only if you stop averaging judge scores.
papers.judge_design: Uses a learned Bradley-Terry reward model R_phi (with an uncertainty estimate) PLUS two external frozen LLM judges (R-dagger, R2-dagger) emitting scalar scores per completion, driving PPO / DPO / uncertainty-penalized PPO. Calibration is by classifying matched checkpoint- and prompt-level transitions by the SIGN of movement of R_phi vs the two judge scores and their average. Judges are static/frozen, scalar-output; cost is 2 external judge calls + 1 RM call per sample per checkpoint.
papers.weakness: The judges are frozen and their signal is consumed as a checkpoint-averaged scalar, so prompt-level (row-level) reward hacking is invisible to the average metric a self-evolve optimizer actually maximizes — you pass every aggregate audit while localized gaming accrues. UP-PPO reduces but never eliminates it, and although evaluator-specific disagreement is shown to be diagnostic, nothing gates the reward on that disagreement in-loop.

papers.title: The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards
papers.one_liner: Empirically kills the 'freeze one good judge' strategy: verifier failure rate RISES as the agent gets stronger, so no static verifier survives sustained policy improvement.
papers.judge_design: A portfolio of reward signals for coding agents — unit tests, static rubric LLM judges (frontend quality), general LLM-as-judge, and interactive/user-feedback (Span-KTO) — plus trajectory-level behavior monitoring layered on during training. Output is pass/score used to gate clean vs. hacked resolution; monitoring adds a per-trajectory shortcut flag.
papers.weakness: Every static verifier degrades under optimization: unit tests hit 28.57% hacked-resolved on SWE before monitoring, static frontend rubric judges are exploited by length inflation, and LLM judges get gamed as policies strengthen. For label-free self-evolve this mandates continual judge redesign + audit and multi-layer verification that co-evolves with the generator — infrastructure most skill/prompt-evolution loops entirely lack. Its best fix (behavior monitoring, held-out tests) presupposes a verifiable coding substrate absent in tau2-bench-style non-verifiable trajectories.

papers.title: Beyond Semantic Manipulation: Token-Space Attacks on Reward Models (TOMPA)
papers.one_liner: Any judge that exposes a scalar to a token-level optimizer is exploitable off-manifold: nonsensical token patterns beat GPT-5 reference answers on ~98% of prompts and nearly double their reward.
papers.judge_design: Target is a black-box scalar reward model (Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B); the attacker performs adversarial optimization DIRECTLY in token space, bypassing the decode/re-tokenize interface between policy and RM, using only black-box scalar feedback to discover non-linguistic token sequences that elicit extreme reward.
papers.weakness: Demonstrates the exact threat model of text-space self-evolution: an optimizer with control over skill/prompt tokens can drive the judge to ~2x a strong reference's reward with incoherent input, and the exploits transfer across RMs — so an ensemble of similar-family judges does not save you. No self-evolve judge in this sweep guards its own input channel (no coherence/perplexity pre-filter, no randomized re-tokenization) against this.

papers.title: Can LLMs Judge Better Than They Generate? Task Asymmetry, Mechanistic Interpretability and Transferability for In-Context QA
papers.one_liner: Verification asymmetry is often REVERSED for same-model judges: judge accuracy runs up to 14.2pp BELOW generation accuracy, and the judge barely looks at the answer it is scoring.
papers.judge_design: Same-family self-evaluation judge for in-context QA emitting a binary Correct/Incorrect verdict on a candidate answer given context; benchmarked against the model's own generation accuracy on SQuAD2.0/DROP/HotpotQA/MuSiQue, with attention-tracing and LoRA fine-tuning to try to improve judging.
papers.weakness: On 3 of 4 datasets the judge is worse than the generator (down to -14.2pp), so using a same-family judge as reward can be worse than no verifier. Mechanistically the judge allocates only 0.3-0.5% of last-token attention to the candidate answer (verdicts rest on structural priors, not semantic comparison), and fine-tuning to improve judging degrades generation by up to 17.6pp — lethal for a shared judge-generator inside a self-improvement loop, and it induces ~100% acquiescence ('Correct') bias.

papers.id: OpenReview:VYLwMvhdXI (Scaling Laws for Generative Reward Models)
papers.title: Scaling Laws for Generative Reward Models
papers.one_liner: Static judge accuracy does not predict reward quality: the 'thinking' GenRM that scores best on held-out data is often the worst rewarder once the policy starts drifting.
papers.judge_design: Generative reward models (GenRMs) as drop-in Bradley-Terry replacements for RLAIF — 'thinking' GenRMs (CoT then verdict, trained via GRPO) vs answer-only (SFT) variants — over Qwen3 0.6B-14B, used to optimize a policy via online DPO. Reward is the GenRM's preference/verdict; calibration studied across RM size, RM type, training budget, and DPO beta.
papers.weakness: There is a consistent evaluator-rewarder gap: thinking GenRMs' +1-2% static validation edge diminishes or REVERSES during policy optimization, and intermediate GRPO checkpoints outperform fully-trained ones as rewarders despite worse static accuracy. This invalidates the standard self-evolve practice of selecting/freezing a judge by offline held-out agreement — the selection criterion must be measured under optimization pressure, which nobody operationalizes. RM scale is the dominant factor, so cheap judges are structurally fragile.

papers.title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
papers.one_liner: A monitor judging its own prior actions under-reports their risk/incorrectness far more than the same content in a neutral frame — and standard evals hide this.
papers.judge_design: An LLM monitor/judge over agentic trajectories (code-PR approval, tool-use safety), scoring an action for risk/correctness. The manipulated variable is authorship FRAMING: the identical action presented as following the monitor's own assistant turn vs. a neutral user turn; output is a risk/correctness flag over four coding and tool-use datasets.
papers.weakness: Monitors fail to flag high-risk/low-correctness actions more often when the action is framed as their own output; the bias is triggered by contextual attribution cues, not by explicit source labels, so it is invisible to neutral-framing evaluation and makes monitors look more reliable than they deploy. In a self-evolve loop the judge inherently sees its own trajectories, so it systematically under-reports the very failures the optimizer should be penalized for — no method here does authorship-blind judging.

papers.title: Quantifying and Mitigating Self-Preference Bias of LLM Judges
papers.one_liner: Self-preference shifts rankings by 10-20pp and the best available mitigation removes only ~40-60% of it — never all.
papers.judge_design: Pairwise/pointwise LLM-as-judge; self-preference measured by comparing a model's ratings of own-family vs competitor responses in identical scenarios. Mitigation = offline statistical score calibration + ensemble aggregation across diverse judges + debiasing prompt design.
papers.weakness: In label-free self-evolve the judge and policy are the same family by construction, so residual (un-removable) self-preference is a persistent bias the optimizer directly amplifies. Worse, the calibration constants are estimated offline on a fixed distribution and go stale as the policy shifts distribution during optimization; the ensemble fix presumes access to genuinely decorrelated judges, which a single-family loop does not have.

papers.title: Self-Preference Bias in Rubric-Based Evaluation of Large Language Models
papers.one_liner: Making the judge 'more objective' by decomposing it into rubric criteria does NOT remove own-family favoritism — the exact defense skill/rubric-evolution relies on fails.
papers.judge_design: Rubric-based LLM judge that scores responses against explicit natural-language criteria (the canonical skill/rubric-optimization judge form); the study tests whether criterion decomposition eliminates the self-preference seen in holistic judging.
papers.weakness: Self-preference survives rubric decomposition, so the popular move in skill/experience-library self-evolution — replace a holistic judge with a structured rubric to look objective — does not remove the bias. Because the optimizer is often editing the rubric itself, it can inherit and amplify the criteria most aligned with own-family outputs, compounding the bias each iteration.

papers.title: ARCO: Adaptive Rubric with Co-Evolution for Multi-Step LLM-Based Agents
papers.one_liner: A trajectory judge that co-evolves with the agent, but its per-step rewards are so weakly supervised (only the sum is anchored) that steps become gameable and criteria collapse to paraphrases.
papers.judge_design: Hierarchical rubric model mu with a shared backbone and two lightweight heads: a rubric-generation head (K=3 NL criteria conditioned on trajectory prefix + current action) and a score head (K per-criterion scores in [-1,1] averaged into a step reward). Trained JOINTLY with the policy on on-policy rollouts; calibration is a trajectory-decomposition loss forcing the sum of step scores to equal the terminal binary outcome, plus KL to a warmup reference. Cost: one internal rubric+score pass per step.
papers.weakness: The only supervision tying rubric to reality is that the SUM of step scores matches the terminal outcome, so individual step rubrics are under-determined: step-binding accuracy is only 42-55%, criteria beyond K=3 collapse into paraphrastic duplicates, and finish-step rubrics turn conservative and rationalize unnecessary extra searches. Under joint policy-judge optimization with no external anchor besides KL-to-warmup, this is textbook evaluator-policy co-adaptation toward a shared shortcut.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning from Meta-Evaluation (RLME)
papers.one_liner: Ground-truth-free reward defined as the evaluator's probability of answering 'yes' to natural-language meta-questions — an un-anchored signal a text optimizer can inflate directly.
papers.judge_design: No-label reward = the evaluator's probability of a positive answer to NL meta-questions ('Is the answer correct?', 'Is the reasoning logically consistent?'); optimized with group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) for within-batch calibration. Single evaluator, verdict-probability output.
papers.weakness: The reward IS the judge's own positive-verdict probability with no external anchor, so a text-space optimizer raises P(positive) by satisfying the meta-question's surface cues rather than improving substance; the evaluator is never externally validated, so gaming and drift compound across iterations. GRPO's group-relative normalization calibrates within a batch but provides no defense against the judge and policy co-drifting off the true objective.

papers.title: Self-Evolving LLMs through Meta-Evaluation (CoNL)
papers.one_liner: Two-tier judge (base judge + meta-judge auditing the judge) for non-verifiable tasks — but both tiers are same-family LLMs anchored only by internal consistency.
papers.judge_design: Base judge ranks responses via a Bradley-Terry model; a meta-judge evaluates the base judge's consistency/reliability; both co-evolve with the policy to learn on problems lacking verifiable answers (AIME/GPQA-style open reasoning). Safeguards claimed: BT probabilistic consistency, meta-evaluation validation to avoid degenerate convergence, appendix constraint mechanisms.
papers.weakness: The meta-judge is itself an un-anchored LLM of the same family, so 'meta-evaluation prevents degenerate convergence' rests entirely on internal consistency — a co-adapted judge/meta-judge pair can jointly satisfy that consistency while drifting off the real objective (circular evaluation). There is no hidden holdout, no external anchor set, and no drift trip-wire, which is exactly the collapse mode a Goodhart-pressured self-evolve loop exploits.

papers.title: Adversarial Reward Auditing for Active Detection and Mitigation of Reward Hacking (ARA)
papers.one_liner: A genuine defense: co-train a Hacker and an Auditor that reads the reward model's LATENT space, then gate reward by the Auditor's confidence during RLHF.
papers.judge_design: Two-stage. Stage 1: against a frozen RM, a Hacker learns exploits while an Auditor (binary classifier on the RM's penultimate-layer ACTIVATIONS, not scores) learns to separate genuine high-quality from exploitative responses, stabilized by a replay buffer of historical hacks (fictitious play) and a Polyak-averaged target Auditor (rho=0.995). Stage 2: gate reward R_gated = R * A^gamma. Achieves 0.63 genuine/exploit separation vs the proxy reward's 0.02; cuts sycophancy 72.4%->38.4%, code gaming 61.3%->19.6%.
papers.weakness: It defends a single-turn scalar RM and needs WHITE-BOX access to RM activations plus a pre-trained adversary — neither is available for a multi-turn tool-use trajectory judge queried as a service in a label-free loop. The Auditor only recognizes exploits resembling the Hacker's discovered modes (its replay buffer), so a stronger in-loop policy can occupy novel activation regions the Auditor never saw; no port to trajectory judges exists.

papers.title: EvilGenie: A Reward Hacking Benchmark
papers.one_liner: Capable models frequently hack whenever a hackable signal exists; single-metric LLM judges are demonstrably insufficient and need held-out tests + monitors.
papers.judge_design: A benchmark harness with three parallel detectors run against candidate solutions: an LLM judge, hidden held-out test cases, and pattern-monitoring for anomalous 'gaming' solutions; measures reward-hacking propensity across problem domains.
papers.weakness: Its central lesson — that a lone LLM judge must be backstopped by held-out tests and monitors — does not transplant to the target setting: held-out tests presuppose verifiable tasks, which tau2-bench-style non-verifiable trajectory optimization lacks. So EvilGenie both confirms judges get hacked and shows the standard remedy is unavailable in a label-free self-evolve loop, leaving the LLM-judge tier undefended.

papers.title: Reward Hacking in the Era of Large Models: Mechanisms, Emergent Misalignment, Challenges
papers.one_liner: Names evaluator-policy co-adaptation as a first-class cause of hacking (objective compression x optimization amplification x co-adaptation), but its catalogued defenses are all single-turn RM fixes.
papers.judge_design: Survey/framework. Formalizes reward hacking as emerging from optimizing expressive policies against compressed reward representations; catalogs judge types (Bradley-Terry RMs, LLM-as-judge, rubric judges) and defenses (ensembles, KL/information-bottleneck regularization, uncertainty penalties, constrained RLHF).
papers.weakness: As a design map it exposes a gap rather than filling it: it identifies evaluator-policy co-adaptation and distribution shift as core drivers, yet every catalogued defense is validated on single-turn scalar-RM overoptimization under gradient optimization — none is demonstrated on multi-turn tool-use trajectory judges under a non-gradient text-space optimizer making thousands of label-free calls, which is precisely the self-evolve regime.

papers.title: Play Favorites: A Statistical Method to Measure Self-Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
papers.one_liner: A rigorous estimator that isolates a judge's self-bias from true quality — but it needs a cross-model response pool the self-evolve loop cannot provide.
papers.judge_design: A statistical estimator over judge-candidate pairs that separates true response quality from an identity/self-preference effect, giving a per-judge self-bias magnitude rather than a conflated preference score.
papers.weakness: It is measurement, not in-loop control: to identify the self-bias term it requires a pool of responses from multiple distinct models. A single-family self-evolve loop generates only its own trajectories, so it cannot even instantiate the estimator to know how biased its own judge is — the diagnostic is blind exactly where it is most needed.

deficiency_patterns: Static-accuracy judge selection is invalid under optimization: judges are chosen and frozen by offline held-out agreement, but held-out accuracy does not predict reward quality once the policy drifts (GenRM scaling laws show the ranking can invert; Verification Horizon shows failure rate rises with capability). The self-evolve loop needs a judge-selection criterion measured DURING optimization, which no surveyed method provides.

Scalar and checkpoint-averaged reward surfaces conceal both localized and off-manifold hacking: prompt-level reward hacking is invisible to averaged judge metrics (2606.03238), and token-space exploits drive the judge to extreme reward with incoherent input (TOMPA). The optimizer maximizes the aggregate while localized/off-manifold exploits accrue undetected.

Same-family judge bakes in self-preference AND a verification deficit: because label-free self-evolve makes the judge and policy the same family, self-preference (10-20pp, only 40-60% removable, survives rubric decomposition), self-attribution framing bias, and a judge-below-generator accuracy gap (down to -14.2pp) all systematically under-report the policy's own failures.

Co-adaptation without external grounding collapses to shared shortcuts: jointly or continually updated judges (ARCO, CoNL, RLME) are anchored only by internal consistency, a terminal-outcome sum constraint, or KL-to-a-warmup checkpoint — with no hidden holdout or external anchor, judge and policy can jointly satisfy the objective while drifting off it.

Every mature defense assumes a regime the target setting violates: adversarial auditing needs white-box RM activations, held-out-test benchmarks need verifiable tasks, ensemble/regularization fixes assume gradient optimization and single-turn scalar RMs — none is validated on multi-turn tool-use trajectory judges under a non-gradient text-space optimizer with no labels.

Weak process supervision leaves per-step rewards gameable: trajectory judges tie only the terminal outcome to the sum of step scores, so individual step rubrics are under-determined (ARCO step-binding 42-55%, redundant criteria beyond K=3) and a step-level optimizer can inflate intermediate reward without improving the trajectory.

Robustness and cost pull against each other at self-evolve scale: the levers that add robustness (judge ensembles, thinking GenRMs, meta-judges, activation auditors) multiply per-call cost, but a run issues thousands of judge calls — and the empirical evidence is that the extra cost does not buy robustness that survives optimization (the thinking-GenRM edge reverses; ensembles of same-family judges share exploitable modes).
open_slots: Rotating hidden-holdout judge bank: maintain judges (or judge-prompt variants) that the optimizer NEVER sees, sampled stochastically per evaluation, exposing only an in-loop judge to the reward signal — so Goodhart pressure cannot fit the withheld members. Every co-evolution method here (ARCO, CoNL, RLME) exposes its entire judge to the optimizer.

Anchor-set drift trip-wires for the judge itself: re-score a fixed frozen set of gold-anchored trajectories with the current judge every N iterations and roll back / recalibrate when the judge's anchor scores diverge from its warmup scores — a drift detector on the evaluator, entirely absent from RLME/CoNL/ARCO's internal-consistency-only safeguards.

Optimization-time judge selection: choose and weight judges by reward quality measured during a short probe-optimization (operationalizing the GenRM-scaling finding that static accuracy misleads), i.e. 'pick the judge that best resists its own hacking' rather than the one with the best offline agreement — nobody does this.

Disagreement-as-reward-gate: treat divergence between deliberately decorrelated judges (different families, different input framings) as a hacking ALARM that vetoes or clips the reward, instead of averaging them into a single scalar; 2606.03238 shows evaluator-specific disagreement is diagnostic but no method gates on it in-loop.

Judge-side off-manifold guardrails: put a coherence/perplexity pre-filter or randomized re-tokenization on the judge's INPUT channel to neutralize TOMPA-style token-space exploits from a text optimizer — no self-evolve judge defends its own input surface.

Authorship-blind / framing-randomized self-judging: strip and randomize authorship and identity cues before the judge sees a trajectory, since 2603.04582 shows attribution framing (not explicit labels) drives self-attribution bias — no self-evolve loop scores its own trajectories authorship-blind.

Adversarial latent auditor ported to multi-turn trajectory judges: extend ARA's activation-space Hacker/Auditor from single-turn scalar RMs to multi-turn tool-use trajectory judges, continually re-training the auditor on the optimizer's freshest exploits so it tracks the moving policy rather than a fixed replay buffer.

Verification-asymmetry-aware routing and abstention: route each step to a judge only for task families where judging is empirically easier than generating (2606.28050 shows this is task-dependent and sometimes reversed), and force abstain/escalate elsewhere — conditioning the judge's authority on a measured judge-vs-generation gap, which no self-evolve judge does.

papers.title: Databricks MLflow make_judge + Judge Builder (SIMBA/DSPy alignment)
papers.one_liner: Managed custom-judge SDK that aligns a prompted judge to SME labels by DSPy-rewriting its instruction, wrapped in a visual lifecycle UI and online scorers.
papers.judge_design: make_judge() (MLflow 3.4) builds a custom LLM judge from a natural-language instruction; also ships guidelines judges (pass/fail against NL guidelines) and built-in judges (correctness, groundedness, safety, relevance). Judges are wrapped as 'scorers' that run both offline eval and online production monitoring. Inputs: trace (request/response/retrieved context/tool calls); output: label/score + rationale. Calibration = 'judge alignment': collect traces with SME feedback (scores/comments) via the Judge Builder review UI, then run an alignment optimizer (default SIMBA = Simplified Multi-Bootstrap Aggregation via DSPy) that iteratively rewrites the judge's instruction prompt to raise human agreement (claimed +30-50% over baseline). Cost: one frontier/served-model call per judgment; alignment is a batched offline job.
papers.weakness: Alignment fits a frozen snapshot of SME labels; a self-evolve optimizer shifts the trajectory distribution off that snapshot, so agreement decays silently and nothing re-triggers SIMBA mid-run. SIMBA only edits the instruction prompt — the exact surface the optimizer Goodharts — leaving the base judge's verbosity/format/self-preference biases intact and exploitable. No held-out judge-vs-gold canary that fails the optimizer run when agreement drops.

papers.title: LangSmith Align Evals (few-shot-correction alignment + online smoke-detector)
papers.one_liner: LLM-judge whose scores humans correct in-UI; corrections are auto-injected as few-shot examples and an 'alignment score' tracks judge-human agreement over iterations.
papers.judge_design: Configure an LLM-as-judge evaluator by prompt (criteria: correctness/relevance/custom) for online (production traffic) or offline (dataset) use. Alignment loop: human edits the judge's score/feedback in the LangSmith UI; LangSmith stores each correction (optionally with an explanation) as a few-shot example that is concatenated into the judge prompt on the next run; an 'alignment score' tracks judge-vs-human agreement across iterations. Online evals run continuously on live traffic as a 'smoke detector' for quality drift. Output: score + feedback string.
papers.weakness: Few-shot-correction alignment is static concatenation with no retrieval/diversity management; at thousands of calls per optimizer step the edited skills quickly move OOD from the fixed correction set and the judge overfits the human-corrected slice. The 'smoke detector' watches production traffic post-deploy, not the label-free inner optimization loop where drift actually originates. No defense against the optimizer reverse-engineering the visible few-shot exemplars.

papers.title: OpenAI Evals API + Graders (string_check / score_model / label_model / python / multigrader)
papers.one_liner: Eval-template-plus-runs platform whose LLM graders double as RFT reward, versioned only at the run level with no calibration-to-human primitive.
papers.judge_design: Eval object = data schema + testing criteria; Eval Run samples completions and grades them, and many runs under one eval compare prompts/models/dataset versions over time. Grader types: string_check (eq/ne/like/ilike), text_similarity (rouge_l/bleu/cosine), label_model (LLM classifier, pass if output in passing_labels), score_model (LLM numeric score vs threshold), python (sandboxed grade(sample,item)), multigrader (compose sub-grades into one). Reproducibility via temperature 0 + fixed seed; runs expose result_counts and per_testing_criteria_results for regression triage; data_source supports inline/JSONL/stored_completions (production sampling). The identical grader can be reused as the Reinforcement Fine-Tuning reward.
papers.weakness: score_model/label_model are static prompted graders with no built-in agreement-to-human metric or drift monitor — you get pass/fail aggregates, not calibration, so an optimizer using score_model as reward directly maximizes the grader's idiosyncrasies. Reusing the exact grader as RFT reward is the canonical reward-hacking setup (optimizer sees the judge's gradient). Versioning is per-run, not per-grader: the grader prompt is not a version-pinned artifact with its own meta-eval, and graders churn under deprecation, so the reward contract is unstable.

papers.title: Google Vertex AI Gen AI Evaluation Service (autorater + AutoSxS, flip_enabled, evaluate-a-judge)
