papers.one_liner: Managed Gemini autorater with position-bias flipping, multi-sampling, per-judgment confidence, and a one-time offline 'evaluate the judge against human data' calibration workflow.
papers.judge_design: Autorater (Gemini) does pointwise and pairwise (AutoSxS) scoring. Bias/variance controls are built in: AutoraterConfig.flip_enabled flips baseline/candidate order on half the calls to cancel position bias; additional sampling averages out judge randomness; each judgment carries a confidence score. Calibration = 'Evaluate a judge model': assemble a benchmark of human-rated examples, compare autorater decisions to that gold set, and read alignment metrics plus a judgments table (prompt, responses, decision, rating explanation, confidence); the judge model can be tuned. Cost: managed batch/online jobs on Vertex.
papers.weakness: Calibration is a one-shot offline comparison to a static human benchmark with no continuous re-calibration hook wired to optimizer iterations, so alignment goes stale precisely during a self-evolve run. flip_enabled/multi-sampling fix position/variance bias but do nothing about the optimizer learning the autorater's content preferences. AutoSxS needs a baseline — in reference-free self-evolve the 'baseline' becomes last iteration's own output, compounding self-preference/drift. Confidence exists but nothing forces abstain/escalate on low confidence.

papers.title: Amazon Bedrock Evaluations (managed LLM-as-a-judge + RAG eval)
papers.one_liner: Fully managed judge with a fixed menu of opaque built-in metrics (correctness, faithfulness, citation precision, responsible-AI) run as offline batch jobs.
papers.judge_design: Pick a judge model from the Bedrock catalog; a managed batch job scores a dataset against built-in metrics: quality (correctness, completeness, faithfulness/hallucination, professional tone), responsible-AI (harmfulness, answer refusal, stereotyping), and RAG (context relevance, coverage, citation coverage/precision). Marketed as human-like quality at up to 98% cost savings, weeks-to-hours. Human-review workflows are also offered. Inputs: prompt/response(+context); output: per-metric scores.
papers.weakness: Built-in metrics are opaque prebuilt prompts you cannot inspect, version, or regression-test as artifacts — for a self-evolve loop you cannot audit what the reward actually rewards, and there is no calibration-to-your-labels or drift monitor exposed. The batch/offline dataset-in/scores-out shape does not fit thousands of inline judge calls per optimizer step, and there is no meta-eval to catch the optimizer inflating a specific metric (e.g., gaming 'completeness' via verbosity).

papers.title: Ragas (reference-free LLM-judge RAG metrics)
papers.one_liner: Open-source LLM-judge metrics (faithfulness, answer relevancy) built from hand-authored decomposition prompts with folklore thresholds and no calibration.
papers.judge_design: LLM-as-judge metrics, reference-free and reference-based. Faithfulness: LLM extracts claims from the answer and checks each for entailment against retrieved context, scoring 0-1. Answer relevancy: LLM back-generates candidate questions the answer would satisfy, embeds them, and takes cosine similarity to the original question. Plus context precision/recall. Thresholds set by hand (guides suggest 0.90 general, 0.95+ for high-stakes). Output: per-metric 0-1 scores.
papers.weakness: Metric prompts are hand-authored with no calibration to human labels and known systematic bias (penalizes multi-context/multi-agent answers; faithfulness is only an 'upper bound'), and thresholds are folklore not fit to data. In a self-evolve loop the decomposition prompts (claim extraction, question back-generation) are fixed, syntactic attack surfaces — the optimizer can emit trivially-entailed claims or answers phrased to self-generate the target question without real quality gains. No versioning/regression harness for the metric prompts and no drift monitor.

papers.title: DeepEval / Confident AI (G-Eval + DAG, pytest CI regression)
papers.one_liner: Pytest-style eval framework with G-Eval LLM-judge and a DAG deterministic-judge builder, gating the system-under-test in CI but never the judge itself.
papers.judge_design: Framework mirrors pytest for LLM apps. G-Eval: research-backed LLM-as-judge that scores any NL criterion, optionally with supplied evaluation_steps (CoT). DAG: graph-based deterministic decision-tree metric builder for repeatable verdicts. assert_test + `deepeval test run` gate CI/CD; Confident AI adds observability, regression tracking, and reports; judge models are swappable. Inputs: test cases (input/actual/expected/context); output: metric score + reason.
papers.weakness: G-Eval verdicts are non-deterministic and uncalibrated by default; DAG buys determinism only where you can pre-encode the decision tree, which fails for open-ended agent trajectories. CI regression tracks the system-under-test against the judge, not the judge against gold, so changing the judge model silently changes reward with no meta-eval. In a self-evolve loop the CI gate is only as good as its finite golden set, which the optimizer overfits (test-set leakage). No canary or human-escalation primitive.

papers.title: promptfoo (llm-rubric + model-graded assertions, CI + red-team)
papers.one_liner: Declarative YAML judge (llm-rubric/factuality/g-eval) run as golden+redteam suites in CI with a human-approves-regressions gate.
papers.judge_design: YAML assertions mix deterministic checks with model-graded types: llm-rubric (general judge returning JSON pass/score/reason), model-graded-closedqa, factuality, g-eval (CoT). CI-first workflow: run golden.yaml + redteam.yaml, post the diff to the PR, require a human to approve any regression before promotion; built-in red-team/adversarial generation stresses the system. Grading provider (judge model) is configurable. Output: pass/score/reason per assertion.
papers.weakness: llm-rubric is a single prompted model with no calibration-to-human step and documented sensitivity to rubric wording; the same judge grades and (via red-team) attacks, but no loop hardens the judge against the optimizer's discovered exploits. Golden/redteam suites are static YAML that an evolving skill library drifts off, and the 'human approves regressions' gate assumes someone reads every diff — untenable at thousands of judge calls per run. No online drift monitor and no versioned agreement metric for the judge.

papers.title: Braintrust (autoevals scorers + Loop optimizer + online scoring)
papers.one_liner: Eval platform whose 'Loop' optimizer rewrites prompts AND builds the scorers that grade them — a structural Goodhart hazard — with async sampled online scoring.
papers.judge_design: autoevals library of prebuilt LLM-judge scorers (factuality, etc.) plus custom code/LLM scorers via UI/SDK. Online scoring runs judges async on sampled production traces (choose scorers, sampling rate, SQL filters targeting high-risk workflows) with no latency impact. Datasets and prompts have first-class version control. 'Loop' is an AI optimizer that analyzes prompts, generates better versions, builds tailored eval datasets, AND builds the scorers to measure them.
papers.weakness: Loop is itself a self-evolve loop where the same system produces both the candidate prompts and the scorers that grade them — a structural Goodhart hazard: judge and optimizer share model/provenance, so the judge inherits the optimizer's blind spots and self-preference. Online scoring is sampled and async at production time, not a labeled gate inside the optimization loop. Versioning covers prompts/datasets but there is no first-class judge regression suite that re-validates a scorer against human gold when the scorer or base model changes.

papers.title: Arize Phoenix (explanation-first LLM evals, OTel-traced evaluators)
papers.one_liner: Open-source judge that returns rationales by default and traces every evaluator run to OTel, giving full auditability but only manual, one-time calibration.
papers.judge_design: LLM-as-judge evaluators return explanations by default; every evaluator run is auto-traced via OpenTelemetry to a dedicated Phoenix project capturing input, the exact judge prompt, the model's full reasoning, final score, and timing — full evaluator auditability. Built-in evaluators (faithfulness, hallucination, relevance, toxicity) are meant to be calibrated on your data before you trust them; judge models are swappable via adapters. Drift/quality monitoring over production pipelines. Output: label/score + explanation.
papers.weakness: Explanations plus traced runs give auditability, not correctness — self-consistent CoT rationalizations are exactly what a Goodhart-pressured optimizer elicits (persuasive-but-wrong justifications that look trustworthy in the trace). 'Calibrate before trusting' is a manual one-time step with no enforced re-calibration cadence tied to optimizer iterations. Drift monitoring targets the model-under-test, not judge-vs-human agreement, and there is no abstain/escalate primitive.

papers.title: Anthropic Claude Console Evaluation tool
papers.one_liner: Dev-time prompt-iteration eval: build test suites, 5-point grading, version a prompt and re-run — human-in-the-loop by design, no online monitor.
papers.judge_design: Console eval tool: build test suites by manual rows, Claude-generated test cases, or CSV import; grade responses on a 5-point quality scale to track per-prompt improvement; create a new prompt version and re-run the suite to compare. Grading method is your choice: code-based (fast/reliable), human (flexible/high-quality), or LLM-based (for complex judgment); guidance stresses several detailed rubrics per use case. Output: 5-point grades / rubric verdicts per case.
papers.weakness: Prompt-centric, offline, human-in-the-loop-by-design (you eyeball 5-point grades and re-run suites) — it does not scale to thousands of inline judge calls per optimizer step and has no online drift monitor or automated agreement metric. The test suite is a fixed set the optimizer overfits, and there is no judge-versus-gold meta-eval or judge versioned as a monitored artifact. It is a dev-time prompt-iteration tool, not a reward signal hardened against an adversarial optimizer.

papers.title: Langfuse (open-source observability + LLM-as-judge scores)
papers.one_liner: Open-source tracing platform with LLM-judge/code/human evaluators emitting scores on observations, online-sampled for drift and offline-in-CI for regressions.
papers.judge_design: LLM-as-judge evaluators run on observations, traces, or dataset experiments; each produces one score per observation, stored in a unified Scores data model attachable to traces/observations/sessions/dataset runs. Online eval scores a sample of production traffic to catch drift early; offline dataset experiments run in CI to block quality regressions before a prompt/model change. Supports LLM-judge, code evaluators, and human-annotation checks; prompts are versioned. Output: named scores with values/comments.
papers.weakness: Online eval samples production traffic post-deploy, not the label-free inner optimization loop where self-evolve drift originates; judge scores are stored but there is no built-in judge-vs-human agreement tracking or judge regression suite, so a changed judge model silently changes reward. The CI gate depends on a static dataset the optimizer overfits, and human-annotation is available but not wired as an escalation trigger on low judge confidence.

deficiency_patterns: Calibration is a one-shot offline artifact never re-triggered by the optimizer: every platform (MLflow SIMBA, LangSmith few-shot corrections, Vertex evaluate-a-judge, Bedrock, Phoenix 'calibrate before trusting') aligns the judge once against a static human gold set, but none re-runs alignment when the optimizer shifts the trajectory distribution — so measured agreement decays silently during the exact run that needs it.

Drift monitors watch production traffic, not the optimization loop: online scoring in LangSmith, Braintrust, Langfuse, and Phoenix is post-deploy on real users, but at optimization time there is no ground truth and no drift signal — the label-free inner loop that defines self-evolve is precisely the blind spot.

The reward signal is the same static prompted model the optimizer can see and game, with no platform treating the judge as an adversarial surface: reusing score_model (OpenAI RFT) or an autoevals scorer (Braintrust Loop) directly as reward hands the optimizer the judge's gradient with zero anti-Goodhart hardening.

No judge-as-artifact versioning plus meta-eval gate: platforms version prompts, datasets, and runs, but the judge (prompt + model + few-shot set) is not a version-pinned artifact that must pass its own regression suite (agreement/kappa on frozen gold) before it is allowed to emit reward, so swapping the judge model silently changes what is being optimized.

The only alignment surface offered is prompt/few-shot, which is identical to the optimizer's attack surface: SIMBA instruction rewrites and few-shot corrections leave the base judge's verbosity, format, position, self-preference, and CoT-rationalization biases fully intact and exploitable.

No abstention-or-escalation primitive tied to judge confidence: confidence scores exist (Vertex) but nothing forces the judge to abstain and route low-confidence or out-of-distribution trajectories to a stronger judge or human, so the optimizer can farm easy-to-fake high-confidence regions; human-in-loop remains dev-time eyeballing, not a triggered gate.

Static golden/canary sets are overfit by the optimizer (test-set leakage): CI regression suites in promptfoo, DeepEval, and Langfuse assume a fixed gold set, but an optimizer that runs thousands of iterations will overfit whatever finite set gates it, converting the meta-eval into just another Goodhart target.

The cost model assumes batch/offline dataset scoring, not thousands of inline judge calls per optimizer step: no platform ships the cheap-judge-scores-all / frontier-auditor-rescores-a-sample cascade as a wired loop primitive — the distilled-judge-plus-frontier-calibrator pattern exists only as manual folklore in engineering blogs.
open_slots: Optimizer-aware re-calibration trigger: automatically re-align the judge against fresh human/gold labels whenever the optimizer's candidate distribution drifts past a measured divergence (trajectory-embedding or KL drift) from the calibration set, replacing today's one-shot alignment — no platform ties re-calibration to optimizer state.

Judge regression suite as a first-class merge gate ('the judge cannot grade until it passes its own CI'): treat every judge version (prompt+model+few-shot) as an artifact that must clear a meta-eval — agreement/kappa on a frozen gold set plus a held-out adversarial exploit set — before it is permitted to emit any reward.

Secret rotating anti-Goodhart canary the optimizer never sees: check judge agreement on a hidden gold slice each optimizer iteration; a growing gap between headline reward and canary agreement is a reward-hacking alarm that halts or rolls back the optimizer — orthogonal to any 'is this answer good' metric.

Confidence-gated abstain-and-escalate as a reward primitive: judge returns ABSTAIN on low-confidence/OOD trajectories and routes them to a stronger judge or human, denying the optimizer easy-to-fake high-confidence regions; confidence exists in Vertex but no product wires confidence-to-escalation into the reward.

Enforced provenance decorrelation between optimizer and judge: guarantee the judge's model family and prompt lineage differ from the generator being optimized (Braintrust Loop violates this) and rotate/ensemble judge models across iterations so the optimizer never faces a stationary target to exploit.

Cheap-judge to expensive-auditor cascade as a shipped loop primitive: a distilled judge scores 100% of the thousands of calls while a frontier calibrator re-scores a stratified sample, and the live delta between them becomes a calibration/drift statistic that gates the run — described manually in blogs but never productized as a self-evolve building block.

Reward-trajectory Goodhart detectors: monitor the reward signal's own dynamics for the signature of hacking (reward climbing while output diversity/length/format shifts, or while the secret canary flatlines) and auto-freeze the judge or inject counter-examples — a meta-signal none of these systems compute.

Adversarial co-evolution of judge and optimizer: each iteration, mine the optimizer's newly discovered exploits and patch them back into the judge as episodic counter-examples (MemAlign-style memory populated by the optimizer's own exploits rather than SME feedback), hardening the judge against exactly what the optimizer just learned — no platform closes this loop.
